sections.markdown:
# Multiple Dispatch

In most OO languages, `obj.method(arg)` dispatches on the type of `obj` only — the receiver. CLOS dispatches on the types of **as many arguments as you specialise**. This is **multiple dispatch**.

```lisp
(defmethod collide ((a asteroid) (b ship))    :ship-vs-asteroid)
(defmethod collide ((a ship) (b asteroid))    :ship-vs-asteroid)
(defmethod collide ((a asteroid) (b asteroid)) :rocks)
(defmethod collide ((a ship) (b ship))         :crash)
```

The applicable method is chosen by combining the specificity of each specialised parameter, with **earlier parameters dominating ties**. So when both args have applicable methods, the one whose *first* specialiser is more specific wins; if those tie, the *second* parameter is the tiebreaker; and so on.

This lets you naturally express operations like geometric intersection, type promotion, and event handling without the visitor-pattern song and dance you'd write in a single-dispatch language.

sections.code: (progn (defclass a () ()) (defclass b () ()) (defgeneric meet (x y)) (defmethod meet ((x a) (y a)) :a-a) (defmethod meet ((x a) (y b)) :a-b) (defmethod meet ((x b) (y a)) :b-a) (defmethod meet ((x b) (y b)) :b-b) (list (meet (make-instance 'a) (make-instance 'b)) (meet (make-instance 'b) (make-instance 'a))))
sections.explain: Argument order matters: (meet a b) and (meet b a) pick different methods.

sections.code: (progn (defgeneric combine (x y)) (defmethod combine ((x integer) (y integer)) :int+int) (defmethod combine ((x integer) (y string)) :int+str) (defmethod combine ((x string) (y string)) :str+str) (list (combine 1 2) (combine 3 "x") (combine "a" "b")))
sections.explain: Methods can specialise on built-in classes too; CLOS picks the right one based on both arguments.

sections.prompt: Define classes ROCK, PAPER, SCISSORS. Define generic function BEATS that returns :LEFT if the first arg beats the second, :RIGHT if the second beats the first, and :TIE if they're the same kind. (Rock>Scissors, Scissors>Paper, Paper>Rock.)
sections.hint: Write nine methods: same-class -> :tie; otherwise figure each pair (e.g. (defmethod beats ((a rock) (b scissors)) :left)).
